records.ety: [OE. callen, AS. ceallian; akin to Icel. & Sw. kalla, Dan. kalde, D. kallen to talk, prate, OHG. kallōn to call; cf. Gr. ghry`ein to speak, sing, Skr. gar to praise. Cf. Garrulous.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Called (kld); p. pr. & vb. n. Calling]
usage: To Call, Convoke, Summon. Call is the generic term; as, to call a public meeting. To convoke is to require the assembling of some organized body of men by an act of authority; as, the king convoked Parliament. To summon is to require attendance by an act more or less stringent anthority; as, to summon a witness.

defs: To command or request to come or be present; to summon; as, to call a servant.
quotes: Call hither Clifford; bid him come amain

defs: To summon to the discharge of a particular duty; to designate for an office, or employment, especially of a religious character; -- often used of a divine summons; as, to be called to the ministry; sometimes, to invite; as, to call a minister to be the pastor of a church.
quotes: Paul . . . called to be an apostle
The Holy Ghost said, Separate me Barnabas and Saul for the work whereunto I have called them.

defs: To invite or command to meet; to convoke; -- often with together; as, the President called Congress together; to appoint and summon; as, to call a meeting of the Board of Aldermen.
quotes: Now call we our high court of Parliament.

defs: To give name to; to name; to address, or speak of, by a specified name.
quotes: If you would but call me Rosalind.
And God called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night.

defs: To regard or characterize as of a certain kind; to denominate; to designate.
quotes: What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common.

defs: To state, or estimate, approximately or loosely; to characterize without strict regard to fact; as, they call the distance ten miles; he called it a full day's work.
quotes: [The] army is called seven hundred thousand men.

defs: To show or disclose the class, character, or nationality of.
quotes: This speech calls him Spaniard.

defs: To utter in a loud or distinct voice; -- often with off; as, to call, or call off, the items of an account; to call the roll of a military company.
quotes: No parish clerk who calls the psalm so clear.

defs: To invoke; to appeal to.
quotes: I call God for a witness.

defs: To rouse from sleep; to awaken.
quotes: If thou canst awake by four o' the clock. I prithee call me. Sleep hath seized me wholly.

defs: To speak in loud voice; to cry out; to address by name; -- sometimes with to.
quotes: You must call to the nurse.
The angel of God called to Hagar.

defs: To make a demand, requirement, or request.
quotes: They called for rooms, and he showed them one.

defs: To make a brief visit; also, to stop at some place designated, as for orders.
quotes: He ordered her to call at the house once a week.

defs: The act of calling; -- usually with the voice, but often otherwise, as by signs, the sound of some instrument, or by writing; a summons; an entreaty; an invitation; as, a call for help; the bugle's call.
quotes: I rose as at thy call, but found thee not.

defs: A signal, as on a drum, bugle, trumpet, or pipe, to summon soldiers or sailors to duty.
An invitation to take charge of or serve a church as its pastor.
A requirement or appeal arising from the circumstances of the case; a moral requirement or appeal.
quotes: Dependence is a perpetual call upon humanity.
Running into danger without any call of duty.

defs: A divine vocation or summons.
quotes: St. Paul himself believed he did well, and that he had a call to it, when he persecuted the Christians.

defs: Vocation; employment.
notes: [In this sense, calling is generally used.]

defs: A short visit; as, to make a call on a neighbor; also, the daily coming of a tradesman to solicit orders.
quotes: The baker's punctual call.

defs: A note blown on the horn to encourage the hounds.
A whistle or pipe, used by the boatswain and his mate, to summon the sailors to duty.
The cry of a bird; also a noise or cry in imitation of a bird; or a pipe to call birds by imitating their note or cry.
A reference to, or statement of, an object, course, distance, or other matter of description in a survey or grant requiring or calling for a corresponding object, etc., on the land.
The privilege to demand the delivery of stock, grain, or any commodity, at a fixed, price, at or within a certain time agreed on.
See Assessment, 4.
